---
title: "TBI"
---

Traumatic Brain Injury
All patients with:
1. Risk of intracranial hypertension (ICP = 22 mmHg) or
2. GCS = 9 and abnormal CT scan or
3. GCS = 9 with normal CT scan and 2 of the following
a. Systolic blood pressure < 90 mmHg
b. Age > 40
c. Decorticate or Decerebrate posturing
Must have the following on admission to the ICU:
1. ICP monitor to be placed within 6 hours
a. EVD or intraparenchymal
2. Cerebral Oximetry Monitor to be placed within 6 hours
a. Parenchymal
3. Invasive arterial monitoring
4. CO monitoring
a. Bedside TTE/hTTE or Flotrack
5. Central venous line and CVP monitoring
6. Bladder temperature
7. ETCO2 Monitor
8. 24 hour EEG within 24 hours of admission
9. Keppra IV infusion
a. 500 mg bid x 1 week
Goals of Management:
1. Maintain CPP = 60 (CPP = MAP – ICP)
2. Core Temperature = 37.5 degrees Celsius
3. ICP = 22 mmHg
4. PbtO2 (cerebral oxygen) = 20 mmHg
5. Target PaCO2 of 35-40 mmHg
6. Sodium goal of 145-150 mmol/L
7. Maintain SpO2 > 90% and PaO2 > 80 mmHg
8. Hemoglobin > 7 g/dL
9. Blood sugar per ICU protocol 100-180mg/dL
10. Ensure adequate sedation to maintain ICP
goals
11. Management of elevated ICP and decreased
PbtO2 using UF severe TBI protocol
Paroxysmal Sympathetic Hyperactivity (PSH)
PSH is a disorder in the regulation of autonomic function
with acute brain injury, most notably severe TBI
 Recurrent episodes of excessive sympathetic
activity that start rapidly, are often induced by
stimulation, and resolve spontaneously after a
variable period of time or after administration
of an abortive medication
 Core clinical features include tachycardia,
hypertension, tachypnea, hyperthermia,
sweating, and/or increased muscle tone with
possible dystonic posturing
 Associated conditions —brain trauma,
nontraumatic forms of acute brain disease,
including anoxic-ischemic coma after cardiac
arrest, intracranial hemorrhage, autoimmune
encephalitis, ischemic stroke, hydrocephalus,
and cerebral fat embolism syndrome


![](images/tbi_page23.png)
